planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in well-drained, average to fertile soil in zone 6–9; Chinese Pistachio is a highly adaptable, medium to large deciduous shade tree native to China, grown primarily for its outstanding, brilliant, reliable autumn color — reds, oranges, and yellows — in climates where Japanese Maples struggle; one of the best autumn-color street and shade trees for the hot-summer, mild-winter South and Southern Plains.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile to poor, alkaline-tolerant soil; Pistacia chinensis is drought tolerant once established and adaptable to clay, calcareous, and compacted soils — an ideal street and parking-lot tree for hot, dry southern climates where few trees provide reliable autumn color. Pistacia chinensis (Chinese Pistachio) is a large, handsome, deciduous tree native to the mountains and valleys of central and western China (Sichuan, Hubei, Shaanxi), a member of the same genus as the edible pistachio nut (P. vera) and closely related to the mastic tree (P. lentiscus). Its primary ornamental value is its outstanding, consistent, brilliant autumn foliage — turning vivid combinations of red, orange, scarlet, and yellow that rival New England maples in color intensity, but dependably in climates from zone 6 through zone 9 including the Deep South, Gulf Coast, and Texas where true maples produce disappointing autumn color. The tree is extremely tough and adaptable: drought tolerant once established, alkaline soil-tolerant, heat-resistant, relatively pest- and disease-free, and tolerant of urban pollution and compacted soils. Small, blue-black drupes on female trees (pistachio-like but not edible commercially) are attractive to songbirds. Chinese Pistachio has become one of the recommended utility and street trees for Texas, Oklahoma, and the Gulf States precisely because of its reliable autumn color and toughness under extreme summer heat and drought.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pistacia chinensis
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Pistacia chinensis

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in moist to wet, well-drained to poorly drained, fertile soil in zone 5–9; Sugarberry (Southern Hackberry) is a large, fast-growing native bottomland tree of the southeastern United States — widespread from Virginia to Florida and west to Kansas and Texas — producing abundant small, reddish-orange to black berries eaten by at least 25 bird species and a wide range of mammals, and one of the most valuable native wildlife trees of the South.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; Celtis laevigata is an exceptionally adaptable native tree tolerating periodic flooding, clay soils, silt, calcareous substrates, and seasonal drought once established — one of the few large trees naturally adapted to Gulf Coast bottomland conditions. Celtis laevigata (Sugarberry or Southern Hackberry) is a large, fast-growing, deciduous native tree of the southeastern United States, ranging from the coastal plain of Virginia and Maryland south through Florida and west to Kansas, Oklahoma, and Texas — a dominant tree of river bottomlands, floodplains, moist woodland slopes, and disturbed sites across the South. The common name 'Sugarberry' references the small, round, thin-skinned, reddish-orange to dark-purple to black fruits (technically drupes) that ripen in late summer and fall — sweet, date-raisin-like in flavor, tiny but edible and historically eaten by indigenous peoples across its range. The wildlife value is extraordinary: the berries are consumed by at least 25–30 bird species including mockingbirds, robins, cedar waxwings, wood thrushes, bluebirds, and yellow-rumped warblers, and by raccoons, opossums, foxes, and white-tailed deer. As a native habitat tree, Sugarberry also supports the larvae of several butterfly species including the Hackberry Emperor (Asterocampa celtis) and Tawny Emperor (A. clyton), whose caterpillars feed exclusively on Celtis foliage. An outstanding choice for native wildlife plantings, riparian buffers, and restoration plantings in the Southeast.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Celtis laevigata
Florida Native Plant Society — Celtis laevigata

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile to average soil in zone 8–11; White Stinkwood is a large, graceful, fast-growing native shade tree of South Africa and eastern Africa, widely planted across the region as a street, park, and garden tree for its elegant, weeping form, light-filtering canopy, and excellent wildlife value — producing abundant small, yellow-orange to reddish drupes eaten by birds and small mammals.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile to average soil; Celtis africana is drought tolerant once established, growing well in a wide range of soils including clay, rocky, and alkaline soils typical of the South African highveld and bushveld — water regularly only in the first year of establishment. Celtis africana (White Stinkwood, Witstinkhout) is a large, graceful, fast-growing deciduous to semi-evergreen tree native to the forests, river margins, and bush-margins of South Africa (particularly the Eastern Cape, KwaZulu-Natal, Limpopo, and Mpumalanga) and extending north through East Africa. The English common name 'stinkwood' refers to the smell of freshly cut wood — the dried wood does not smell strongly and is actually a fine-grained, attractive timber used for furniture and flooring in South Africa (though far less valuable than the true 'Black Stinkwood,' Ocotea bullata). Celtis africana is one of South Africa's most popular garden shade trees: fast-growing, with an elegant, slightly weeping branch structure, fine-textured, lime-green foliage that provides deep shade, and outstanding wildlife value — the small, round, yellow-orange to red-black fruits are eagerly eaten by many fruit-eating birds including barbets, mousebirds, bulbuls, and starlings. The tree supports many butterfly species and is larval host plant for the Charaxes butterfly group.
sources: Walter Sisulu National Botanical Garden — Celtis africana

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in deep shade or bright indirect light in zone 9–12; Rainbow Moss (Peacock Moss) is a creeping, mat-forming, fern ally native to moist forests and rock faces of southern China and Taiwan — one of the most visually stunning groundcovers available for shaded positions, with scale-like, iridescent, blue-green-purple foliage that shifts color like a soap bubble in changing light.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, consistently moist but well-drained soil in deep to moderate shade; Selaginella uncinata requires high humidity (above 60%) and consistent soil moisture — it dries out and browns quickly in dry conditions; excellent for enclosed shade gardens, terrariums, vivaria, and humid greenhouse floors. Selaginella uncinata (Rainbow Moss, Peacock Moss, or Aurora Selaginella) is a delicate, mat-forming, creeping fern ally (Selaginellaceae — the spike mosses) native to the moist, shaded forest floors, rocky outcrops, and stream margins of subtropical southern China (Guangdong, Guangxi, Fujian) and Taiwan. The plant is celebrated for its extraordinary iridescent foliage: the minute, overlapping, scale-like leaves are structurally colored like a peacock feather — the surface microstructure diffracts light to produce a shifting blue-green-purple-copper iridescence that changes tone as the viewing angle changes, unlike any color produced by pigment. This optical effect — analogous to the structural coloration of butterfly wings and peacock feathers — is the product of thin-film interference in specialized leaf surface cells. The resulting groundcover appearance is unlike any other plant: a shimmering, metallic, multi-colored carpet perfect for deep shade terrarium displays, forest-floor groundcover in humid tropical gardens, and naturalistic shade vivaria. Maintain high humidity, consistent moisture, and protection from direct sun and cold drafts.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Selaginella uncinata

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in deep shade to partial shade in zone 8–11; Tuberous Sword Fern is a robust, vigorous fern native to tropical Asia and East Africa, widely naturalized in warm climates worldwide, distinguished from the common Boston Fern by small, round, white storage tubers on its stolons — highly drought tolerant for a fern because of these tubers — and one of the best large, shade-tolerant groundcovers for hot, humid southern landscapes.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in shade to partial shade; Nephrolepis cordifolia is considerably more drought tolerant than Boston Fern due to its water-storing root tubers — once established it tolerates periods of dry soil that would wilt typical ferns. Nephrolepis cordifolia (Tuberous Sword Fern or Ladder Fern) is a robust, clumping, stoloniferous fern native to tropical and subtropical Asia, East Africa, and the Pacific Islands, now widely naturalized in Florida, the Caribbean, Central America, Hawaii, and New Zealand — where it has become invasive in some regions due to its vigorous growth and prolific stolon production. The plant is distinguished from the very similar common Boston Fern (N. exaltata) by small, round, white, marble-sized tubers borne on the stolons — starchy, edible in some regions as bush food — which function as water-storage organs allowing the plant to survive drought periods that would desiccate typical ferns. The pinnate fronds are erect to arching, bright green, 1–2.5 feet long, on multiple crowns that spread and colonize by stolons. Tuberous Sword Fern is an excellent, tough, large-scale shade groundcover for hot, humid, subtropical gardens in zones 8–11, performing well under trees, on shaded slopes, and in difficult dry-shade situations where most ferns fail. Monitor for spreading in warm climates where it can become invasive.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Nephrolepis cordifolia
Pacific Island Ecosystems at Risk (PIER) — Nephrolepis cordifolia

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moist soil in partial shade to full sun in zone 7–11; Florida Coontie is the only cycad native to the continental United States — a small, slow-growing, drought-tolerant landscape groundcover native to Florida and the Caribbean, critically important as the SOLE larval food plant of the Florida Atala butterfly (Eumaeus atala), a species nearly extirpated by over-collection of Coontie for starch; now widely planted for Atala butterfly conservation.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Zamia integrifolia is extremely drought tolerant once established — the large underground starch-storing caudex enables survival through months-long droughts; avoid overwatering or clay soil with poor drainage. Zamia integrifolia (Florida Coontie, Comptie, or Florida Arrowroot) is the only cycad species native to the continental United States, occurring naturally in scrub, pine flatwoods, coastal hammocks, and rocklands of peninsular Florida, the Florida Keys, and parts of the Caribbean. The plant is a small, compact, low-growing cycad — virtually stemless, with glossy, dark-green, finely divided pinnate fronds emerging from a buried or partly exposed starchy underground caudex — forming attractive, groundcover-size clumps 1–3 feet tall. The starchy caudex was extensively processed by indigenous Floridian peoples (Timucua, Seminole) and later by European settlers as 'Florida arrowroot' — a nutritious, starch-flour used for bread, porridge, and starch products, driving commercial Coontie starch factories in early 20th-century Florida until over-harvesting nearly extirpated the plant from the wild. Today, Florida Coontie is critically important as the only larval food plant of the Florida Atala butterfly (Eumaeus atala), a spectacular iridescent blue butterfly nearly lost when Coontie populations collapsed; its recovery depends directly on Coontie restoration. An outstanding, low-maintenance native landscape plant for Florida gardens.
sources: Florida Native Plant Society — Zamia integrifolia
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Florida Cycads
